baseline_dominance.justification: Construction claims cryptographic issuer-hiding with constant-size proofs, which BBS+ cannot match on proof size (O(n) group elements) and RFC 7662 cannot match without AS-as-TTP. However, rfc7662_advocate Attack 1 shows the NCUA ECIES escrow is structurally equivalent to the AS TTP — trust is relocated, not eliminated. The construction never argues why the escrow has strictly smaller disclosure surface than a filtered-introspection AS. Additionally, the 'constant-size + arbitrary schema' claim is unsubstantiated (auth0_pm Attack 2, cryptographer Attack 3): no universal circuit mechanism or predicate-depth bound is specified, so the key differentiator over BBS+ is aspirational. Baseline can approximate with RFC 7662 claim-filtering + PPID + DPoP for most scenarios.

formal_security_argument.justification: Threat model exists: verifier as issuer-hiding adversary. Named assumptions: Groth16 knowledge soundness, Poseidon collision resistance, ECIES IND-CCA2. Two formal games defined (dIND-ISS §3.2 with distributional adversary, MUNL §3.3 with epoch-pinning). However, critical adversarial oracles are missing from both games: (1) dIND-ISS does not model corrupt-escrow oracle — cryptographer Attack 1 shows escrow+verifier collusion wins with advantage 1; (2) MUNL does not model corrupt AS with scope_id knowledge — cryptographer Attack 4 shows nullifier precomputation deanonymizes within-epoch; (3) neither game models registry timing — cryptographer Attack 2 shows revocation fingerprinting narrows anonymity sets. Reduction sketch is absent. Games are defined but incomplete.

implementability.justification: Construction maps to existing Bolyra primitives (Poseidon hash, Groth16 circuit, BabyJubjub EdDSA, nullifier scheme) and Section 6 provides wall-clock benchmarks on 6 platforms including constraint counts. However, the core claim — 'constant-size predicate circuit that handles arbitrary Boolean expressions over claim schemas' — has no specified mechanism: no universal circuit depth parameter D, no SNARK-friendly IR compiler, no folding protocol (auth0_pm Attack 2, cryptographer Attack 3). Benchmarks appear to cover only the single-attribute demo case (chartered_by_NCUA == true), not worst-case predicate complexity. Circuit cost for the general case is unknown.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 codex adversary with 5 personas found multiple attacks the construction cannot answer. Most critically: cryptographer Attack 1 (ECIES escrow collusion breaks dIND-ISS with advantage 1 — the escrow ciphertext in the proof transcript lets a colluding verifier+escrow trivially recover issuer identity, defeating the construction's central security claim). Cryptographer Attack 4 (corrupt AS precomputes nullifier table for all enrolled agents within an epoch, deanonymizing any proof — 360 hash evaluations). Cryptographer Attack 3 (arbitrary-schema claim is an impossibility without specified universal circuit). cu_ciso found 4 out-of-threat-model attacks on escrow key custody, MPC ceremony vetting, member key recovery, and revocation SLA. The escrow collusion attack is fatal: the construction mandates a mechanism that trivially defeats its own security game.

scenario_fit.justification: Three scenarios named with regulatory citations (31 U.S.C. § 5318(h), GLBA § 314.4(a), NCUA Part 748). However, spiffe_engineer Attack 4 demonstrates that for scenarios 1 (cross-CU NCUA) and 2 (cross-firm FINRA), SPIFFE federation suffices and issuer-hiding is nice-to-have, not load-bearing. Only scenario 3 (cross-country KYB with jurisdiction hiding) strictly requires issuer-hiding, but it lacks a named stakeholder or specific regulatory mandate — it is described generically as 'cross-country KYB proof where jurisdiction must stay hidden' without identifying a concrete deployment, institution, or jurisdiction-specific regulation. No scenario names a specific credit union, regulator office, or procurement engagement where this property is load-bearing.

gaps: FATAL: dIND-ISS game must include a corrupt-escrow oracle — current design lets verifier+escrow collude to recover issuer with advantage 1. Requires redesign: either designated-verifier proof, oblivious escrow (e.g., threshold ECIES where escrow requires k-of-n to decrypt), or escrow ciphertext routed through a separate channel not observable by verifier.

MUNL game must model corrupt AS as adversarial oracle with scope_id knowledge. Nullifier derivation must include per-user blinding factor not known to AS to prevent within-epoch precomputation attack.

Specify the universal circuit mechanism for arbitrary-schema predicates: state maximum predicate depth D, the circuit structure (universal Boolean evaluator vs. folding/recursion), and benchmark worst-case predicate complexity — not just single-attribute demo.
Argue why ECIES escrow has strictly smaller disclosure/subpoena surface than an always-online AS with claim-filtering policy. Without this, baseline dominance claim is a trust-topology rearrangement.

Extend MUNL/dIND-ISS to model registry timing side channel: adversary who observes on-chain batch timestamps can narrow issuer anonymity set. Either prove epoch-pinning still holds or move to private registry (Merkle commitment with ZK membership proof).
Add proof-size comparison column to Section 6 benchmarks: proof bytes vs. BBS+/SD-JWT for equivalent predicate complexity to substantiate constant-size claim.
Name a specific credit union, regulator, or procurement engagement for the cross-country KYB scenario where jurisdiction-hiding is a regulatory requirement, not just desirable.
Address escrow key custody: who holds the ECIES private key, under what contractual/regulatory framework, with what audit trail — cu_ciso's examiner will require this for GLBA § 314.4(b) compliance.
